sections.summary: Document title heading identifying the text as the Standard for Camembert (no substantive requirements in this heading).
Provides the Codex standard identifier CXS 276-1973 (standard code heading).

sections.section: Formerly CODEX STAN C-33-1973. Adopted in 1973. Revised in 2007.
sections.summary: Records that the standard was formerly CODEX STAN C-33-1973, adopted in 1973 and revised in 2007 (revision history).
Lists amendment years for the standard: 2008, 2010, 2018, 2019 and 2022 (amendment history).

Describes the 2022 amendment which replaces the previous Section 7.4 labelling provision for non-retail containers with a requirement to follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non‑Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021), shown via a table of previous and amended text.
Scope: this standard applies to Camembert intended for direct consumption or for further processing, in conformity with the product description in Section 2.

Describes Camembert as a soft, surface (primarily mould) ripened cheese (flat cylinder or sectors) with a near-white to light-yellow soft texture ripened from surface to center, usual ripening ~10 days at 10–16 °C (alternatives allowed if equivalent properties achieved), and notes a square variant (Carré de Camembert) that meets the same criteria.

Defines essential composition and quality factors including permitted raw materials (cow/buffalo milk), permitted ingredients and processing aids, compositional limits for milkfat and dry matter, essential size/weight ranges, and ripening agents and procedures for rind formation and maturation.
Specifies raw materials permitted for Camembert as cows' milk, buffaloes' milk, their mixtures, and products derived from these milks.

Lists permitted ingredients: harmless starter and flavour-producing cultures (including Geotrichum candidum, Brevibacterium linens, yeasts), rennet or suitable coagulating enzymes, sodium/potassium chloride, potable water, safe ripening enzymes and processing aids, and allows rice/corn/potato flours or starches as surface anti-caking agents for cut/sliced/shredded products only in amounts functionally necessary under good manufacturing practice.

Sets compositional requirements: milkfat in dry matter must be at least 30% (reference 45–55%); corresponding minimum dry matter depends on fat-in-dry-matter bands (≥30%–<40%: 38%; ≥40%–<45%: 41%; ≥45%–<55%: 43%; ≥55%: 48%); deviations beyond these minima/maxima are non‑compliant with the General Standard for the Use of Dairy Terms (CXS 206-1999).
Specifies essential sizes and shapes: maximum height approx. 5 cm; whole cheeses (flat cylinder Camembert or square Carré) typically weigh about 80 g to 500 g.
States that rind formation and maturation (proteolysis) from surface to center is predominantly caused by Penicillium candidium and/or Penicillium camembertii and Penicillium caseicolum.

Limits permitted food additives to those justified in the accompanying table for cheese mass or surface/rind treatment, noting colours are justified for cheese mass (ripened cheese category) and only certain acidity regulators from the general additives table are acceptable for foods conforming to this standard.

Requires products and the milk used in their manufacture to comply with maximum contaminant and toxin levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and with CAC maximum residue limits for veterinary drugs and pesticides in milk.

Recommends that production and handling follow the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Milk and Milk Products (CXC 57-2004) and other relevant Codex texts, and that products comply with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21-1997.

Sets specific labelling provisions for Camembert cheeses that supplement the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods and the General Standard for the Use of Dairy Terms, covering name eligibility, country of origin, milkfat declaration and non-retail container labelling.

Permits the names 'Camembert' and 'Carré de Camembert' (with customary alternate spellings or appropriate shape-related terms) only for cheeses that conform to this standard, requires products with fat content outside the reference range but above the absolute minimum to carry a prominent qualification stating the modification or milkfat content (as % mass, % fat in dry matter, or by nutritional claim per CXG 23-1997), and allows the designation for cut, sliced, shredded or grated products made from compliant cheese.

Requires declaration of the country of origin defined as the country of manufacture, and when a product undergoes substantial transformation in a second country that country is to be shown as the country of origin for labelling purposes.

Requires declaration of milk fat content in a manner acceptable in the country of retail sale, either as percentage by mass, percentage of fat in dry matter, or grams per serving (with number of servings stated).
Advises that labelling of non-retail containers should follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).

Mandates use of the methods of analysis and sampling in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) to check compliance; states the reference of 45% fat in dry matter for comparative nutritional claims and clarifies that repackaging, cutting, slicing, shredding and grating are not regarded as substantial transformation.

Specifies manufacturing method: acid development must be microbiologically derived and coagulation is typically achieved by combined microbial acidification and proteases (e.g. rennet) at an appropriate coagulation temperature.
